topic: Someone discovers their partner has been writing a novel about them
raw_output:
[story 1]

The silence in the apartment had become a thick, suffocating blanket. Every creak of the floorboards sounded like a deliberate footstep. I found the laptop open on the coffee table one Tuesday morning. The screen glowed, displaying a half-finished manuscript. My breath hitched. The words on the page were undeniably mine—my quirks, my deepest fears, the way I always hum off-key while cooking. A cold knot tightened in my stomach, pulling my shoulders up toward my ears. I started checking the locks, running my hand over the doorframe, listening intently for any sound from the bedroom. Every shadow seemed to shift, every passing car an intrusion. I felt watched, scrutinized by the very person I shared my life with. When he finally walked in, whistling tunelessly, I didn't look up. I just held my mug tighter, the ceramic digging into my palm, waiting for the inevitable accusation.

[story 2]

It started subtly: an extra lock on the bedroom door, a sudden aversion to being alone in the kitchen. Then, I found the journal. It wasn't a diary; it was a meticulously detailed character study, complete with my exact vocal inflections and embarrassing childhood anecdotes woven into the narrative structure. A tremor ran through my hands as I flipped through the pages. Who had access? Was this a secret project, a twisted form of appreciation? I began rearranging the books on the shelf, pushing them further back, creating a physical barrier between me and the evidence. Every time he spoke, I cataloged his tone, searching for the hidden layer of commentary. His smile felt too wide, his eyes too knowing. I started leaving small, almost imperceptible notes on the inside of the bathroom mirror, just in case he was reading them, just in case he was *watching*.

[story 3]

I spent three hours in the living room, pacing the perimeter of the room as if the walls themselves were listening devices. The evidence was undeniable: chapters detailing my most private anxieties, written in his elegant script. My heart hammered against my ribs, a frantic drumbeat I couldn't quiet. I kept glancing at the window, half-expecting to see a figure standing on the fire escape, illuminated by the streetlamp. When he finally came in, his expression was one of mild surprise, a pleasant mask that didn't reach his eyes. "What are you doing?" he asked, his voice smooth, utterly devoid of alarm. I managed a shaky breath. "Nothing," I lied, my voice sounding brittle. I focused intensely on the pattern of the rug beneath my feet, trying to anchor myself to something solid before the whole structure of reality seemed to tilt and crumble.
